text: A eight-year-old man was admitted in emergency for a four-hour onset of acute abdominal pain. Previous medical history was consistent with six months non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs used for chronic abdominal pain.
Physical examination disclosed acute peritoneitis with diffuse abdominal guarding and rebound turdiness.
Hemodynamic status was normal. with slight tachycardia.
Emergent CT scan showed three air bubbles next to a jejunal tract.
The surgeon performs an emergent laparoscopic exploration of the abdomen using a three-port approach, providing a view of the abdominal cavity for further examination.
Laparoscopic exploration reveals localized inframesocolic acute peritonitis, with visible small bowel content and fibrin deposits within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon performs a complete laparoscopic exploration of the small bowel, revealing a covert perforation in a solitary diverticulum located in the proximal jejunum.
The surgeon suspends the jejunal tract to the abdominal wall using percutaneous sutures, preparing for resection of the involved segment.
The surgeon uses an endo-GIA linear stapler to transect the small bowel, visibly dividing the jejunal tract.
The surgeon uses a ligature Maryland to divide the mesentery, maintaining a distance of two centimeters from the bowel wall.
The surgeon positions the two small bowel stumps side by side in an isoperistaltic orientation and suspends them to the abdominal wall using sutures.
The surgeon performs a side-to-side jejuno-jejunal intracorporeal anastomosis using an endo-GIA linear stapler, which is inserted through two small enterotomies in the bowel.
The surgeon closes the enterotomies using a free or resolvable barbed running suture, ensuring the edges of the bowel are securely approximated.
The surgeon applies a first extra-mucosal suture layer followed by a second seromuscular suture layer to reinforce the anastomosis site on the small bowel.
The surgeon places a reinforcing non-absorbable free-hole monofilament suture at the lower edge of the anastomosis site and closes the mesenteric defect, ensuring secure closure of the surgical area.

The surgeon performs a thorough lavage of the abdominal cavity using two liters of saline solution. A 24 French silicone drain is visibly placed within the cavity to facilitate postoperative drainage.
The surgeon extracts the resected jejunal tract into an endo bag and visually inspects the tissue for any abnormalities or issues.
Post-operative course was uneventful and the patient was discharged at home on the sixth post-operative day.
